texts.orig: Figure 11: Frequency of glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) measurements in chronic kidney disease (CKD) and non-CKD patients.
texts.text: Figure 11: Frequency of glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) measurements in chronic kidney disease (CKD) and non-CKD patients.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Practice Point 2.1.1: Monitoring long-term glycemic control by HbA1c twice per year is reasonable for patients with diabetes. HbA1c may be measured as often as 4 times per year if the glycemic targget is not met or after a change in glucose-lowering therapy.
texts.text: Practice Point 2.1.1: Monitoring long-term glycemic control by HbA1c twice per year is reasonable for patients with diabetes. HbA1c may be measured as often as 4 times per year if the glycemic targget is not met or after a change in glucose-lowering therapy.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Chapter 2 An important feature of the drug is the ability of the drug to raise blood glucose levels. The drug is able to raise blood glucose levels by increasing the amount of glucose in the blood. This is because the blood glucose levels are higher than the levels of glucose in the urine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Practice Point 2.1.5: For patients with T2D and CKD who choose not to do daily glycemic monitoring by CGM or SMBG, glucose-lowering agents that pose a lower risk of hyperglycemia are preferred and should be administered in doses that are appropriate for the level of eGFR.
texts.text: Practice Point 2.1.5: For patients with T2D and CKD who choose not to do daily glycemic monitoring by CGM or SMBG, glucose-lowering agents that pose a lower risk of hyperglycemia are preferred and should be administered in doses that are appropriate for the level of eGFR.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Patients with diabetes and more advanced CKD stages are at increased risk of hypoglycemia. Selecting glucose-lowering agents with very low or no hypoglycemia risk should be considered, especially for patients who cannot perform hyperglycemia testing. Patients with very low or no hypoglycemia risk should be considered, especially for patients who cannot perform hyperglycemia testing. Patients with very low or no hypoglycemia risk should be considered, especially for patients who cannot perform hyperglycemia testing.
